slug: 如何用数学证明你的直觉是错的_贝叶斯定理
title: 如何用数学证明你的直觉是错的——贝叶斯定理

outline.concept: Base rate neglect: your intuition automatically focuses on how reliable the evidence seems, but silently drops how common the outcome was before you saw any evidence. A 99% accurate test for a disease that affects 1 in 10,000 people still means a positive result is almost certainly wrong — your gut refuses this.
outline.query: 直觉 判断 准确率 阳性 概率 忽略 常见 base rate 先入为主

outline.concept: False positive trap: when a condition is rare, the huge pool of healthy people each generating a small chance of false alarm produces far more false positives than the tiny pool of sick people produces true positives. The numerator (true positives) is small; the denominator (all positives) is flooded with noise.
outline.query: 体检 报告 阳性 误诊 担心 假阳性 准确率 false positive 测出

outline.concept: Prior strength governs how much evidence moves you: a strong prior (something very likely or very unlikely) demands extraordinary evidence to overturn; a weak prior flips on modest evidence. One bad review changes your mind about a restaurant you've never heard of, but not about your favorite one.
outline.query: 改变看法 第一印象 信任 偏见 经验 累积 一次 反转 prior belief

outline.concept: The corrective habit is always asking 'how common was this before I got the evidence?' — not replacing intuition with math, but adding the one question your intuition systematically skips. Most probability errors collapse once you retrieve the base rate.
outline.query: 反思 质疑 直觉 以为 其实 先验 问自己 多常见 冷静 reconsider
